questionText: The statement $x = y \Rightarrow x^2 = y^2$ is true for all real numbers $x$ and $y$. Write down the converse of this statement, and determine whether the converse is true, giving a counterexample if it is not.

steps.description: Recall what the converse of an implication is.
steps.explanation: The converse swaps the roles of hypothesis and conclusion. It is a genuinely different statement, so its truth has to be checked separately from the original.

steps.description: Label the hypothesis and conclusion of the original.
steps.workingLatex: P: x = y, \qquad Q: x^2 = y^2
steps.explanation: Naming the two halves makes the swap mechanical. Here $P$ is the assumption and $Q$ is the consequence in the original implication.

steps.description: Write the converse by exchanging P and Q.
steps.workingLatex: Q \Rightarrow P: \quad x^2 = y^2 \Rightarrow x = y
steps.explanation: Swapping $P$ and $Q$ gives the converse, which now claims that equal squares force equal numbers. This is the statement we must test.

steps.description: Decide how to test the converse.
steps.workingLatex: \text{seek } x, y \text{ with } x^2 = y^2 \text{ but } x \ne y
steps.explanation: An implication fails only when the hypothesis holds yet the conclusion does not. So we look for numbers with equal squares that are nevertheless unequal.

steps.description: Choose a pair of opposite numbers.
steps.explanation: A number and its negative always share the same square, which makes such a pair the natural place to look for a counterexample.

steps.description: Check that this pair breaks the converse.
steps.workingLatex: x^2 = 1,\ y^2 = 1 \Rightarrow x^2 = y^2; \quad 1 \ne -1 \Rightarrow x \ne y
steps.explanation: The squares agree, so the hypothesis $Q$ holds, but the numbers differ, so the conclusion $P$ fails. This single case is enough to break the implication.

steps.workingLatex: x^2 = y^2 \Rightarrow x = y \ \text{ is false}
steps.explanation: Because a counterexample exists, the converse is false even though the original statement is true. An implication and its converse can have different truth values.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Converse: $x^2 = y^2 \Rightarrow x = y$. It is false; for example $x = 1,\ y = -1$ give $x^2 = y^2$ but $x \ne y$.
